Te Mana Roranga, the Māori Data Sovereignty Network, brings together more than
800 Māori researchers, practitioners, and entrepreneurs who are passionate about
data and Māori rights. As a group focused predominantly on advocacy,
Te Mana Roranga is different to Te Kāhui Roranga Charitable Trust,
who are party to the Mana Oraite Agreement.
However, we support the submission on this bill made by the Data Iwi
Leaders Group. Te Mana Roranga also engages with the Global
Indigenous Data Alliance, supporting the realization of global indigenous
data rights through the care principles and supporting UNDRIP and
other international and national treaties and conventions.
Today, representing Te Mana Roranga are three members of Te Whakapū, the
steering group. Dr Lara Greaves, an associate professor in politics at
Victoria University of Wellington with expertise in Māori politics, survey
development, quantitative methods in Māori and LGBTQ+
demography. Dr Logan Hamley, a senior researcher at Whakawai Research
Services, a Ngāti Hauāz lead research center.
And Larissa Renfrew, a PhD student at the University of Auckland who has conducted
research related to Māori data sovereignty and ethics, Māori identity, youth
wellbeing, and demography.
Collectively, our group has also used the RDI in research, and also been
consulted by Stats NZ about the approach to
LGBTQ+ topics in the census.
TMR's position on this bill is shaped by our understanding of Māori data as
both a strategic asset and a living taonga to Māori.
This includes both data produced by the census or the new proposed
approach. Our position is also formed by the principles of Māori data
sovereignty as foundational to Te Mana Roranga's advocacy work.
Te Mana Roranga welcomes the opportunity to submit on this bill, and we want to
begin by acknowledging that there are genuine positives in the proposed model.
More frequent data releases will be valuable.
The flexibility to tailor surveys for different subpopulations is
promising, and we commend the focus on meeting the needs of communities, including
Iwi Māori. But we have serious concerns, and we believe this
legislation is premature. The move to administrative census
removes the enumerated census before we know the replacement works.
Experts across the board have raised grave concerns about data quality, and
Statistics New Zealand's own independent review has indicated that high-quality
statistics from administrative data will not be achievable by
For Māori, the stakes are particularly high.
Administrative data is deficit-framed.
It is collected at the most vulnerable moments in people's lives and does not tell
the full story of our communities.
Critical variables like Māori descent, Iwi affiliation, and te reo
Māori fluency have no clear plan still.
And Māori descent is not just a data question.
It underpins our electoral system and our constitutional arrangements.
There is also a deeper issue of data sovereignty and consent.
This bill expands and links administrative data without free,
prior, and informed consent, and without adequate Māori governance, and
without upholding the Mana Oraite Agreement.
Aotearoa has the opportunity to build a world-leading trustworthy data system,
and this bill, as it stands, moves us in the wrong direction.
Yeah. And as for our recommendations, we kind of considered, okay, so
there's been this process, it's been quite a long consultation process, a
methodological process within Stats NZ, and we can definitely
see some flaws in that process and really want to know what
is the best model going forward. How can we make this work considering costs,
considering the various issues we've discussed today, and that various other
speakers will discuss today? We think that an independent review of the
methods, of the governance, of the decisions made so far is
really our recommendation, and making sure that there are-...
reviewers with expertise in Māori data sovereignty, Māori data
governance. We see a real missed opportunity here to create
our own model, our own data ecosystem.
Not just copying a Nordic model, but making something unique
to Aotearoa, New Zealand that brings in Te Kahui
Turoa's Māori data governance model, which has some great features for
everyone, but would also uphold Tititi and do great
in terms of building a trustworthy data system, a robust data system, and
one that just works better for everyone and ultimately saves a lot of
money. We think it's great that the bill moves towards greater
engagement than just every five years with Māori, but the way that
administrative data works is that decisions are made within agencies
and made within Stats NZ that need greater review and
greater engagement with Māori. Because we know for population,
someone will make a decision that just seems like a little technical tweak
here and there, but it can have a lot of effect on someone's day-to-day
life or how they interact with the state and the system.
So ultimately, we really think that this is a missed opportunity to
create a great data system that governs
our data as New Zealanders and as Māori, as
taonga, upholds Māori data sovereignty, governs data as
taonga, but also as a national strategic asset.
So ultimately, we side with a lot of the experts that are saying there are great
methodological issues, but also point to the suite of governance
issues as well with this move away from the enumerated
census. Kia ora. Thank you for listening.
Good. Thank you very much. Now, I've got a couple of questions, or three actually.
Camilla Belich first.

oral submission and your written submission.

look in terms of its falseness? Do you have any concerns
around
that type of result that might come out of the use of

Mm. And Māori descent.

Mm

Descent Indicator.

even if you

Okay. So I'm Dr. Helen Figgers in place of Professor Peter
Crampton, and presenting on behalf of the team that has created the New Zealand
Deprivation Index for every census from nineteen ninety-one to twenty
twenty-three. We used data from the full enumeration census
of population and dwellings to quantify the relative socioeconomic
deprivation of small areas, generally only one to two hundred people,
often SA1s, across New Zealand. NZDep
was initially created to help with resource allocation in the health sector, but
has also found a wide range of use in other sectors, like ECA and
community law centers.
NZDep is useful precisely because it has an intensely place-based
measure that identifies where people, family, and households experience
relative socioeconomic deprivation.
The full enumeration census of population and dwellings has also been
intensely place-based, tying the characteristics of dwellings,
households, and people to the areas in which they live.
There are two main replacements to
the
full enumeration census, the Census Attribute Survey and the Admin
Population Census. So firstly, the proposed Census Attribute
Survey is place-based, but it won't count everyone, and it will
average
results over much larger areas than NZDep uses.
Only those with five thousand people or more.
This means no information available at the SA1 level and no
information available for almost all SA2s, and only for
about half of SA3s.
So it's not going to be useful for the creation of future NZDeps.
Pockets of socioeconomic deprivation, sometimes only one or two blocks
long, can exist inside well-off areas like Karori, and
similarly, well-off pockets can exist inside poorer locations.
And capturing this small area diversity is one of the really
important uses of NZDep.
The other thing, the Administrative Population Census.
This will aim to capture everyone, but admin data is collected at
the person level and is not intrinsically place-based.
Connections to place have to be worked out, and as recently as March this

of only eighty-seven percent. Similarly, although Stats New
Zealand is investigating how to calculate families and households from
person data, it cannot yet adequately do so.
Also in March, it reported a calculated household consistency
of only sixty-eight percent.
Families and households are vital subgroups of society, and people's
experience in them is part of their socioeconomic experience.
And so they're important in an NZDep.
There are also quality issues with admin data.
The information will only be as timely as the person's last interaction with
government services. This means for some people, it's going to be wildly out of
date or nonexistent, and for others, extremely current.
Whether or not it's out of date could also be socioeconomically patterned,
and the accuracy of data will be affected by people's willingness to
share information with the different services and the computer systems and
programming within which it's stored.
Overall, of the nine variables we used to calculate NZDep
twenty twenty-three, only three were at the person level and
are already confirmed to be available in some way inside the
Administrative Population Census.
The other
six at the family, household, and dwelling levels may or
may not become available. Some probably will, others almost
certainly won't. We currently don't know.
And we don't know how good the quality and coverage of any variables that do
become available will be.
So these issues will negatively affect the production of any future
NZDep-like index.

replacement data is of adequate quality, coverage, and
granularity. And for these reasons, we oppose this bill.
If the bill is passed, then we urge the committee to ensure adequate funding for

And we also recommend that in the very early twenty-thirties, there's a
review to implement it to investigate whether or not the data is
actually suitable for New Zealand's data needs, and if
not, a plan to revert to a full enumeration census.

as you

recommendations on this bill that there needs to be
kind of mandatory transparency around when AI is being used?
Absolutely. I think if you look at the IDI in particular,
there are a lot of opportunities, and there's a lot of data in there that
is and could be used further and in more
advanced ways than we're currently using it.
However, I think the challenge we've got, which we've always
had, and so it's not just unique to New Zealand, is around
social license. And I think if we are
going to leverage the capabilities we've already invested in, and
public investments already gone into a lot of this, is
greater transparency means there's going to be more uptake,
hopefully more voluntary
sharing of information, and better acknowledgement and awareness
from the public for why this is really important.
At the moment,
if you look at the census responses or many other things, is
the lack of public engagement is it's, yes, it means we
haven't told the value proposition of why data is
so important and so instrumental to how governments make
good decisions.
But at the same time, is we haven't actually
clearly made it easy for public to engage and see
how their data is being used and how they can actually contribute to the
decisions that shape the country.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
No worries. Thank you so much for your time.
Relatively short submission, but very powerful, so thank you for that.
It was. Thank you very much. I appreciate your time this afternoon.
Yeah. They're not available?
Lance Smith and Zunza are being rescheduled for the afternoon.
Okay, so we're now going to call a hold.
We'll come back at 1:00,
so we'll back into it with a vengeance.
Thank you
